title: How to start and end a thread cleanly
subtitle: Loop start and away knot: the two anchoring methods that leave a tidy back.
excerpt: A knot on the back of a counted-thread piece is bulky, shifts under the fabric, and shows through the front as a bump. Stitchers anchor the thread two other ways: a loop start for two-strand work and an away knot for any thread count.
sourceNotes:
Methods drawn from two public-domain references:

• Therese de Dillmont, Encyclopedia of Needlework (1886), §Stitches used in embroidery. Project Gutenberg.
• Caulfeild & Saward, The Dictionary of Needlework (1882), entry for Knot. Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method for anchoring a new thread by folding one strand of stranded cotton in half, threading the cut ends through the needle, and catching the folded loop on the back of the first stitch. No knot.

A method for anchoring a new thread by placing a small knot on the front of the cloth at a distance from the working area. The thread is cut and unpicked once the working stitches have anchored its tail on the back.

content.text: A knot on the back of a counted-thread piece is bulky, shifts under the cloth as the piece is washed and pressed, and shows through the front of the finished work as a small bump. Embroiderers anchor the thread two other ways. The choice depends on how many strands are on the needle.

Once a few stitches are worked, the thread tail is held on the back by the working stitches themselves. The job of the start method is only to hold the thread for the first three or four stitches. The end method is the same on the back of the piece: run the tail under three or four worked stitches and trim.
Loop start (two strands)
The
loop start
is the cleanest anchor for stitching with two strands (14-count cross-stitch) or any even number of strands.
Cut one strand of stranded cotton at twice your working length (about 80 cm).
Fold the strand in half so it doubles into two strands joined by a loop at one end.
Thread the two cut ends through the eye of the needle. The loop is now at the far end, away from the needle.
Bring the needle up through the cloth at the start position, leaving the loop just below the back of the cloth.
Work the first half of the first cross-stitch (the bottom diagonal). As the needle goes back down through the cloth, pass it through the loop on the back. Pull firm.
The thread is now anchored without a knot. Carry on stitching.
Away knot (any thread count)
The
away knot
works for any number of strands, including a single strand on 18-count cloth where the loop start doesn't apply.
Thread the needle with the strands you'll be stitching with. Tie a small knot at the cut end.
Push the needle down through the cloth from the front, about 5 cm away from where you'll start stitching, in a direction the stitching will travel towards.
Bring the needle up at the start position and begin stitching. Each stitch passes over the thread on the back of the cloth, anchoring the tail.
After three or four anchoring stitches, snip the knot off the front of the cloth and pull the cut tail through to the back. Run it under a couple more stitches on the back and trim flush.
Ending a thread

When a working thread runs low (with about 10 cm left on the needle), end it on the back of the cloth. Pass the needle under the back of three or four worked stitches in the same colour, pull through, then trim the tail flush. Don't carry the thread across an open area of cloth at the back: the dark trail shows through pale fabric, and the tail can catch as later stitches go in.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on the back
attrs.intro: Three habits that show up on the front of a finished piece if the back isn't tidy.

items.symptom: A knot bump shows through on the front
items.cause: A knot was used to start (or end) on the back of the cloth. The bump pushes the front fabric out slightly.
items.fix: On the next piece, use a loop start or an away knot. On the current piece, undo the offending start, pull the thread out, and re-anchor with one of the cleaner methods.

items.symptom: A long dark thread shows through the cloth at the back of a pale area
items.cause: A thread was carried across an unstitched area of cloth on the back to start a new colour patch.
items.fix: End the thread at the last stitch of the previous colour, snip, and start a fresh thread when you reach the next colour patch. Carrying a thread is acceptable across two or three squares between stitches of the same colour, but never across an unstitched area.

items.symptom: A new thread comes loose after a few stitches
items.cause: The loop start didn't catch the loop properly on the back, or the away knot was placed too close and the thread isn't yet anchored.
items.fix: Pull the thread out, re-thread, and try again. For the loop start, make sure the needle passes through the loop on the back as it goes down for the first stitch's second leg. For the away knot, leave it at least 5 cm from the start position so three or four anchoring stitches lay over the tail before you snip the knot.

content.text: Where this goes next

Loop start and away knot are the two foundation anchoring methods. The same logic carries to surface embroidery, blackwork, and hardanger: knots stay off the back. Once a few starts and ends are behind you,
the cross-stitch itself
is the next thing to practise.
